commits.messageBody:
Three coupled defects let per-session `tokenUsage` reset to zero on reopen
and drift away from its parts within a session:

- The Session constructor never read `SessionRecord.tokenUsage`, so the
 live `_tokenUsage` counter restarted at zero on every reopen after
 eviction or process restart.
- Per-turn increments lived only on `_tokenUsage`; nothing overlaid the
 counter into the next `_flushUpdate` write, and turns without a
 follow-up save never persisted at all.
- `_recordTurnCompletion` only updated `totalTokens` when the provider
 emitted one. Providers that only return `inputTokens` / `outputTokens`
 left the total stale relative to its parts.

Fixes:

- Seed `_tokenUsage` from `internals.record.tokenUsage` in the
 constructor, coercing each field through `?? 0` so partially-populated
 legacy rows do not poison subsequent `+=` into `NaN`.
- Overlay `tokenUsage: { ...this._tokenUsage }` into both the in-flight
 CAS save and the post-save record assignment in `_flushUpdate`, so every
 write carries the latest counter even when concurrent increments arrive
 while the save awaits storage.
- Schedule a trailing no-op `_flushUpdate(prev => prev)` from
 `_recordTurnCompletion` so increments persist even when no other state
 change follows the turn. Non-lifecycle storage failures latch onto
 `_pendingTokenUsageFlushError` so shutdown and tests surface them via
 `_internalAwaitFlushChain()` instead of silently dropping data.
- Reorder `_finalizeCompletedQueuedTurn` to call `_recordTurnCompletion`
 before `_markQueuedPostRunFinalized`, so the no-replay marker's CAS
 write piggybacks the new counter via the overlay. A crash between the
 two writes would otherwise resume with the marker set and never
 re-account the turn.
- Derive `totalTokens` from `(prompt ?? 0) + (completion ?? 0)` when the
 provider omits it, leaving the explicit-totalTokens branch untouched.
- Have `harness.shutdown` await each live session's flush chain before
 releasing the lease so the trailing persist lands before a fresh owner
 hydrates.

Tests in `token-usage.test.ts` cover the constructor seed path, the
shutdown+rehydrate roundtrip, the queued-turn marker/accumulator
ordering, and four cases of the `totalTokens` derivation branch.
commits.messageHeadline: fix(core/harness): persist token usage across rehydration

commits.messageBody:
`harness.deleteSession(...)` removed storage rows but never asked the
workspace provider to clean up when the session was no longer live in
`_perSession` (closed-but-not-resumed, or evicted in a previous harness
generation). For per-session resumable providers that keep on-disk state
between releases, the per-session directory leaked on every delete that
happened outside the original Session's lifetime.

Fixes:

- Add `WorkspaceRegistry.releaseDormant(opts)` that materializes the
 workspace through `provider.resume({...persistedState})`, runs
 `provider.destroy(workspace, ctx)` (or `workspace.destroy()`), and emits
 the matching `workspace_status_changed` / `workspace_error` events.
 Idempotent via an in-flight `_dormantReleasing` map; resolves to a
 no-op for sessions still live in `_perSession`, for non-per-session
 configs, and when no persisted state is on the record.
- Have `acquirePerSession` await any in-flight `_dormantReleasing` promise
 before resuming so a concurrent acquire never collides with a dormant
 resume+destroy on the same session.
- Throw `HarnessWorkspaceProviderMismatchError` from `releaseDormant`
 when the persisted `providerId` does not match the configured one, and
 let the error propagate out of `_deleteClosedTree` to abort the delete.
 Swallowing here would erase the breadcrumb the original provider needs
 to clean its filesystem state; the operator must reconfigure the
 matching provider and retry.
- Call `releaseDormant` from `_deleteClosedTree` for every per-session
 node in the bottom-up tree before storage rows are removed. Errors
 other than the mismatch error are absorbed (the registry already
 emitted `workspace_error`).

Tests in `workspace-registry.test.ts` cover the dormant teardown path
across two harness generations, the no-double-destroy invariant when the
workspace was already released on close, and the mismatch-aborts-delete
invariant (record stays so the operator can recover).
commits.messageHeadline: fix(core/harness): tear down dormant per-session workspaces on delete

commits.messageBody:
Today, optional Harness storage features (`appendWorkspaceActionJournalEntry`,
`appendSessionEvent` / `getSessionEventReplayState` / `listSessionEvents`)
advertise support by throwing per-feature `Unsupported*Error` from the
base class. Every consumer that wants to know whether the feature is wired
has to call the method and `try/catch`. Route handlers, the Session
runtime, A2A, channels, and release-time compatibility tooling can't
distinguish "feature not configured" from "feature failed at runtime."

Fixes:

- Add `HarnessStorageCapabilities` (`base.ts`): a structural map with seven
 boolean keys — `workspaceActionJournal`, `sessionEventReplay`,
 `admissionConflictDetection`, `attachmentBlobs`, `channelOutbox`,
 `wakeups`, `proactiveWakeups`. Static; callers may cache.
- Add `HarnessStorage.capabilities(): HarnessStorageCapabilities`. Default
 uses the same prototype-detection idiom as `supportsAtomicDeleteSessions`:
 a feature family counts as supported only when EVERY method in the
 family is overridden on the adapter's prototype. The five abstract
 families (admission, attachments, channelOutbox, wakeups, proactive)
 default to `true` because any concrete adapter implements them.
- Wire `session._enqueueSessionEventPersistence` to short-circuit up front
 when `capabilities().sessionEventReplay` is false. The legacy `instanceof`
 catch on `HarnessStorageSessionEventReplayUnsupportedError` stays as
 defense-in-depth.
- Wire `session._recordWorkspaceAction` to short-circuit up front when
 `capabilities().workspaceActionJournal` is false. Emits the one-shot
 `workspace_action_journal_unsupported` event and returns without
 attempting the append. Existing `instanceof` catch retained.

Why prototype detection rather than declaring booleans by hand: the libsql
and pg adapters live in separate packages and already override every
method in both optional families. With prototype detection their
`capabilities()` automatically reports `workspaceActionJournal: true` and
`sessionEventReplay: true` without any change to those packages. Adapters
with config-driven toggles override `capabilities()` directly.

Tests:

- `capabilities.test.ts`: InMemoryHarness reports all-true; a fixture that
 reassigns optional methods back to the base prototype reports the
 matching feature as unsupported; a half-implemented family (write but
 not read) reports unsupported; explicit `capabilities()` overrides take
 precedence.
- `workspace-runtime.test.ts`: a `NoJournalAdapter` whose `capabilities()`
 returns `workspaceActionJournal: false` emits the one-shot unsupported
 event AND never calls `appendWorkspaceActionJournalEntry`. A
 `NoReplayAdapter` whose `capabilities()` returns `sessionEventReplay:
 false` never calls `appendSessionEvent`.

Out of scope:

- Boot-time capability sanity check.
- Release-matrix generation from `capabilities()`.
- `libsql` / `pg` adapter capability overrides — covered automatically by
 the prototype-detection default.
- The third family of unsupported errors (`HarnessStorageProviderCallbackBindingUnsupportedError`,
 `HarnessStorageChannelDiagnosticsUnsupportedError`,
 `HarnessStorageThreadDeleteFenceUnsupportedError`) — same pattern is
 trivial to add once consumers need declarative probes.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness): declare storage capability matrix as runtime API

commits.messageBody:
…ycle

Today a `session.message(...)` turn that spawns a background process via
the `execute_command` workspace tool (`sandbox.processes.spawn(...)` with
`background: true`) leaves that OS process alive when the session is
later closed, evicted, or hard-deleted. The sandbox process manager
already tracks live handles, but the Harness layer has no signal that
fires on session lifecycle, so accumulated background processes leak
across the host as long-running MastraCode deployments run.

Foreground commands are safe — their lifetime is already bounded by the
turn's `abortSignal` via the sandbox process manager's abort-to-kill
wiring (see process-manager.ts:81-97).

Fixes:

- Add `registerBackgroundProcess?: (handle: ProcessHandle) => () => void`
  to `HarnessRequestContext`. Tools that spawn long-lived processes call
  it via `context.requestContext.get('harness').registerBackgroundProcess(handle)`.
- Add `Session._backgroundProcesses`, a Symbol-keyed map of registered
  handles. Symbol keys avoid an OS-pid-reuse race where a stale
  `unregister` callback could delete a newer registration that happens
  to reuse the same OS pid.
- Add `Session._registerBackgroundProcess(handle)`. In `live` / `closing`
  states it stores the entry and wires `handle.wait().then(unreg, unreg)`
  so normal exits and wait() rejections both clean up. In any terminal
  state it kills the handle immediately and returns a no-op unregister
  so a register-arrives-after-close race cannot leak.
- Add `Session._reapBackgroundProcesses()` invoked at the top of
  `_markClosed`, `_markEvicted`, and `_markDeleted`. Snapshots the map,
  clears it, then issues fire-and-forget `handle.kill()` per entry.
  Idempotent.
- Wire the slot method in `_buildRequestContext` alongside the existing
  `recordWorkspaceAction` slot.
- `execute-command.ts` background branch: after `sandbox.processes.spawn`,
  optionally call the harness slot's `registerBackgroundProcess`. The
  call is best-effort — tools outside the Harness runtime see no slot
  and the registration is a no-op.

`Session.abort()` does NOT call reap. Background processes spawned by
the active turn already use `context.abortSignal` (the turn's
controller), and the sandbox process manager wires `abortSignal` to
`handle.kill()` natively. Reaping on abort would additionally kill
long-lived watchdog processes spawned by previous turns, which is
destructive and outside the minimal-first scope.

Out of scope for this commit:
- Persisting tracked pids to storage (cross-harness reap).
- A `persistAcrossEviction` opt-in for dev-server-style background
  processes that should outlive eviction.
- A public `background_processes_reaped` event (deferred to a follow-up
  to avoid session-event-persistence ordering concerns at `_markEvicted`
  / `_markDeleted` paths).

Tests in `background-process-reap.test.ts` cover registration,
auto-unregister on both successful exit and `wait()` rejection, reap on
close / evict / delete, no-op when no processes registered, the
foreground-not-tracked invariant, the race-window kill-on-register-after-
close path, and a regression guard for the OS-pid-reuse race that
motivated the symbol-keyed map.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness): reap orphan background processes on session lifec…

commits.messageBody:
Today the Harness runs a periodic heartbeat at TTL/3 (min 1s) that renews
each live session's storage lease. Under event-loop starvation (CPU-bound
JS) or a tool that holds the session past the default TTL, the heartbeat
can miss its deadline and another Harness instance can take over the
session via `acquireSessionLease`. The CAS contract on `saveSession`
rejects late writes from the original owner, but the window is observable
and tools have no way to push the storage expiry forward ahead of work
they KNOW will exceed the default TTL.

Fixes:

- Add `Session.extendLease(opts: { ttlMs: number }): Promise<void>` and a
  convenience wrapper `Session.withExtendedLease(fn, opts)`. Tools that
  expect to exceed the default lease TTL can push the storage expiry
  forward before the blocking work; subsequent heartbeat renewals respect
  the extension via `_getEffectiveLeaseTtlMs`.
- Validate `ttlMs`: must be a positive integer; reject non-finite,
  non-integer, ≤0, and values above `MAX_LEASE_EXTENSION_MS` (24h cap).
- Clamp `ttlMs` upward inside the chained step against (a) the harness
  default TTL and (b) the remaining extension window. A second
  `extendLease(shorter)` while a longer extension is still active must
  NOT shrink the lease.
- Per-session lease-write serialization via `_leaseRenewalChain`. Both
  `extendLease` and the heartbeat per-session arm queue their work on the
  same chain so a default-TTL heartbeat in flight cannot overwrite a
  longer extension. Mirrors the `_flushChain` idiom used elsewhere.
- Lifecycle guard on the chain: queued renewers become no-ops once the
  session is no longer `live`/`closing`. Prevents the heartbeat (or a
  late chain entry) from re-extending a lease for a session the harness
  has already terminalized.
- Clear `_leaseExtensionDeadline` on `_markClosed` / `_markEvicted` /
  `_markDeleted`.
- Expose `extendLease` on the `HarnessRequestContext` slot so tools call
  it via `context.requestContext.get('harness').extendLease({...})`.
- Refactor `_renewLiveSessionLeases` to route per-session renewals through
  `session._enqueueLeaseRenewal(...)` so a concurrent `extendLease`
  cannot race with the heartbeat against `storage.renewSessionLease`.
  Effective TTL is computed inside the chained step so it reflects the
  latest extension deadline.
- Add `Harness._internalLeaseTtlMs` getter for the Session-side clamp.

The existing `session_evicted` event with `reason: 'lease_lost'`
(events.ts:86) already covers the observability requirement for
lease-loss; no event taxonomy expansion in this commit.

Out of scope:
- Persisting the extension deadline to storage (in-memory only; on
  restart the lease reverts to whatever storage has).
- Auto-extension based on turn duration heuristics.
- Cross-platform lease semantics changes.

Tests in `session.lease.test.ts` cover argument validation, storage-side
expiry bump, deadline bookkeeping, no-shrink-against-default,
no-shrink-against-existing-extension (regression), heartbeat-preserves-
extension via the chain, effective-TTL semantics under all branches
(default, longer-than-default, expired, non-finite), the convenience
wrapper, the terminal-state guard for queued renewers, and a two-Harness
takeover scenario where an extended lease blocks `HarnessSessionLockedError`.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness): lease-extension API + heartbeat coordination

commits.messageBody:
Today `_runGoalJudge` catches every exception from `_callJudge` and emits
`goal_paused` with the catch-all `reason: 'judge_failed'`. `_callJudge`
casts the judge's structured output without validating against the
verdict schema, so a malformed verdict that happens to type-check loosely
can fall through as `'continue'` and keep the loop enqueuing turns based
on a nonsense decision.

Fixes:

- Define a four-way `GoalJudgeFailureKind` taxonomy
  (`'timeout' | 'provider_error' | 'invalid_verdict' | 'max_turns'`) on
  `storage/domains/harness/types.ts`, along with a `GoalJudgeFailure`
  record `{ kind, message?, failedAt }` persisted on
  `GoalState.lastFailure` so a recovered session can introspect what
  went wrong.
- Replace `GoalPausedEvent.reason: 'judge_failed'` with three taxonomy
  variants: `'judge_timeout'`, `'judge_provider_error'`,
  `'judge_invalid_verdict'`. The legacy union member is retired.
  Subscribers can now distinguish a transient timeout from a non-
  transient provider error from a malformed verdict.
- Add `HarnessGoalJudgeFailedError` carrying `kind`. Thrown from
  `_callJudge` after schema validation or classification; caught by
  `_runGoalJudge`.
- `_callJudge` now runs `GoalJudgeSchema.safeParse(full.object)` and
  throws `HarnessGoalJudgeFailedError({ kind: 'invalid_verdict' })` on
  schema failure. Invalid verdicts can no longer fall through as
  `'continue'`.
- `_callJudgeWithRetry` retries `'timeout'`-class failures once with a
  1s backoff before giving up. `AbortError` name OR message matches
  for `timeout|timed[ -]out|ECONNRESET|ETIMEDOUT` qualify as timeout
  class; everything else is `'provider_error'` and fails fast.
- `_runGoalJudge`'s catch path classifies the thrown value into a
  `GoalJudgeFailure`, stamps it on the goal record via `_flushUpdate`,
  and emits `goal_paused` with the reason mapped from the kind.
- Successful judge verdicts clear any prior `lastFailure` via a
  destructure-strip so a pause-then-resume-then-success cycle leaves
  the goal in a clean state instead of carrying stale failure metadata.
- The existing budget-exhausted branch now stamps
  `lastFailure.kind: 'max_turns'` so the introspection surface is
  uniform across all pause paths.

Out of scope (per the issue's tightened scope):
- Silent queue-full backpressure on goal continuations (covered by a
  separate durable-queue-scheduling issue).
- Changes to the judge's prompt or context-building.

Tests in `session.goal-judge-taxonomy.test.ts` cover: AbortError →
retry → pause with `judge_timeout` + `lastFailure.kind: 'timeout'`;
network-timeout error message → retry; generic Error → no retry, pause
with `judge_provider_error`; `HarnessGoalJudgeFailedError('invalid_verdict')`
→ pause with `judge_invalid_verdict` + regression guard that the pending
queue stays empty (no fall-through to `'continue'`); `max_turns`
exhausted → `lastFailure.kind: 'max_turns'`; pause-then-resume-then-
success clears `lastFailure`; happy-path `'done'`/`'continue'`/`'waiting'`
unchanged. The pre-existing `session.goal.test.ts` "judge throws" test
is updated to assert the new `judge_provider_error` reason and the
populated `lastFailure`.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness): goal-judge failure mode taxonomy

commits.messageBody:
Today every `spawn_subagent` invocation creates a fresh child session via
`harness.session(...)` with `origin: 'subagent-tool'` and only `close()`s
it in the finally block. `close()` flips lifecycle state but does NOT
delete storage rows, so heavy fan-out workloads (parallel explore
subagents, large review/migration runs, or any remote/A2A path that
spawns many short-lived subagents per task) accumulate closed-but-
undeleted session rows indefinitely.

Fixes:

- Add `retain?: boolean` to `SubagentDefinition`. Default `false`
  (ephemeral). When `true`, the spawned subagent session is preserved
  across `subagent_end` for re-attach scenarios.
- In `spawn-subagent-tool.ts` main `finally` block, AFTER
  `parent._emitSubagentEvent({type:'subagent_end'})` (so subscribers see
  the event before the row vanishes), if `def.retain !== true` then
  best-effort `await harness.deleteSession({sessionId, resourceId}).catch(()=>{})`.
- Mirror the same cleanup at the workspace-tool-construction early-
  return error path so a failed spawn does not accumulate closed
  children when the same workflow fans out hundreds of them.
- Update the spawn-subagent test setup helper to default `retain: true`
  for the test subagent type because the existing test suite inspects
  the child session row after the tool returns to verify configuration
  (mode/model/parent linkage); under production default `retain: false`
  the row would be gone before those assertions could run. New tests
  that exercise the ephemeral-cleanup contract explicitly pass
  `retain: false`.

KNOWN GAPS (deferred to follow-ups; documented for operators):
- `deleteSession` does NOT cascade to thread/message rows.
  `harness.threads.delete(...)` is the right surface for that, but
  layering it on top of the existing override-aware MemoryStorage
  guards needs its own commit. Today's commit cleans the session row;
  the cloned thread (forked path) and the freshly-minted thread
  (default path) plus their message rows remain until a follow-up.
- Forked-thread cleanup when `threads.clone()` succeeds but
  `harness.session()` then throws is not handled (no child exists).
  Narrow window; will land with the thread-cleanup follow-up.
- TTL sweep for crashed subagents (never receive `subagent_end`).
  Separate commit; orthogonal to the inline-cleanup contract here.
- Per-call `spawn_subagent({retain:true})` override — only the
  per-type SubagentDefinition flag exists today.

Tests in `session.spawn-subagent.test.ts` cover: default ephemeral
cleanup deletes the row; `retain: true` preserves the row + closedAt;
`subagent_end` event still emitted before cleanup; workspace-tool
construction error path cleans the row (regression for codex review
finding); bulk 25 sequential spawns leave zero child rows under that
parent.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness): subagent session retention policy

commits.messageBody:
The Harness storage layer and `workspace-policy.ts` evaluators already
accept `'network'` and `'mcp'` as `actionKind` values, but the
classifier in `workspace-actions.ts` only ever produced `'file'` /
`'command'` kinds. MCP and network tool calls therefore escaped both
the durable workspace-action journal and the policy engine — every
`<serverKey>_<toolName>` MCP invocation and every URL-bearing tool call
slipped past the audit surface.

Fixes:

- Extend `HarnessWorkspaceActionKind` to `'file' | 'command' | 'network'
  | 'mcp'`, matching the storage type and the policy evaluator. Widen
  the `workspace_action_journal_unsupported` event's `actionKind`
  union to match.
- Add `mcpServerKeys?: readonly string[]` to
  `classifyHarnessWorkspaceToolAction`. Tool names shaped
  `<key>_<rest>` resolve to `actionKind: 'mcp'` with
  `{ serverId, toolName: rest }`. Longest-prefix match wins so
  overlapping keys (e.g. `weather`, `weather_eu`) classify
  deterministically.
- Add lightweight network detection: when no file/command/mcp match,
  inspect the tool args for a top-level `url` / `endpoint` / `uri`
  string. Parse via `new URL(...)` and journal as `'network'` with
  `host` / `port` / `protocol` / `url` / `method?` payload. Default
  ports for `http`/`https`/`ws`/`wss` are filled in when omitted.
- Restrict network classification to host-bearing network schemes
  (`http`/`https`/`ws`/`wss`). Schemes like `file:`, `data:`,
  `mailto:`, `blob:` parse cleanly through `new URL()` but their
  host/port payload is meaningless, so they are NOT journaled as
  network actions (regression guard added for this codex finding).
- Wire `mcpServerKeys: harness._listMcpServerKeys()` through
  `Session._mapWorkspaceToolAction`. Add a thin `_listMcpServerKeys()`
  helper on `Harness` that reads the same registry already used by
  `_listMcpServers()`.

Classifier precedence is: static file/command descriptor > MCP
namespace match > network URL match > undefined. Static descriptors
keep their existing semantics — no regression on file/command tools.

Out of scope (separate follow-up):
- Policy enforcement of `network` / `mcp` allow/ask/deny rules
  (Linear ticket's second sub-deliverable; lands after the journaling
  half).
- Nested URL fields in args (e.g. `args.config.url`) — only top-level
  `url` / `endpoint` / `uri` are inspected.
- IPv6 literals and non-standard ports beyond what `new URL` natively
  supports.

Tests in `workspace-actions.test.ts` cover: MCP namespace match
(simple, longest-prefix, no-match, empty-suffix rejection, mixed-content
key list); network URL detection via `url` / `endpoint` / `uri` aliases,
default ports per scheme, HTTP method capture, non-string url rejection,
unparseable url rejection, non-network-scheme rejection (codex
regression); classifier precedence (file/command beats MCP, MCP beats
network). Pre-existing file/command behavior is unchanged.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness): classify network and MCP workspace actions

commits.messageBody:
…/mcp actions

`workspace-policy.ts`'s `evaluateWorkspacePolicy` was exported but never
invoked from runtime. Every workspace-action journal entry recorded the
caller-passed `policyDecision` verbatim with a stock reason and empty
matchedRules — operators had no way to observe whether a tool call
respected a configured allow/ask/deny policy, and the new `network`/`mcp`
classifier kinds (the previous commit) carried no policy verdict at all.

Fixes:

- Add `policy?: WorkspacePolicy` to every variant of `HarnessWorkspaceConfig`
  (`shared`, `per-resource`, `per-session`).
- Store the policy on `Harness._workspacePolicy` and expose via the
  `_internalGetWorkspacePolicy()` accessor.
- In `Session._recordWorkspaceAction`, if a policy is configured AND the
  classifier output translates cleanly via `_toPolicyAction(mapped)`,
  call `evaluateWorkspacePolicy(policy, policyAction)` and overlay its
  decision + reasons + (JSON-sanitized) matched rules onto the journal
  entry.
- `_toPolicyAction` strict guards (per codex review):
  - file: only the policy's `WorkspaceFileOperation` set
    (`read`/`write`/`delete`/`rename`/`patch`); the classifier's
    already-resolved absolute path is preferred over the raw input so
    nested policy roots (e.g. `/ws` + `/ws/readonly`) cannot mis-match a
    relative tail (codex regression test added). `toPath` resolved the
    same way; rename actions carry both.
  - command: requires `mapped.action.command` to be a non-empty string —
    skips `get_process_output`/`kill_process` and similar shapes that
    don't fit the policy's command action.
  - network: requires a non-empty string host; port flows through only
    when a finite number, protocol only when a string.
  - mcp: requires a non-empty serverId; toolName is optional.
- Caller-passed `params.policyDecision` is preserved on the journal entry
  under `actor.callerDecision` so operators can compare caller intent vs.
  policy verdict on every row.
- When a policy is configured but the action can't be mapped (e.g.
  `get_process_output`), the runtime falls back to the caller-driven
  decision and tags the entry with
  `harness.policy_unmappable_<kind>_<operation>` so the surface gap is
  visible.

Behavior change is evidence-only: the runtime does NOT block tool calls
based on the policy verdict. Gating is a separate behavior change tied
to the permission-profiles ticket.

Tests in `session.workspace-policy.test.ts` cover: file deny rule
overlay (with the absolute-path anchoring); the nested-roots regression
from the codex review; command actions without a string command fall
back to caller decision with the
`harness.policy_unmappable_command_read_output` reason; network deny
rule overlay; an unrecognized MCP-shaped tool name does not journal
(the integration with a registered MCP server is documented as a
follow-up); no policy configured = legacy behavior unchanged.

Changeset: patch on `@mastra/core`.

commits.messageBody:
…ssion

The previous subagent-retention commit cleaned the closed session row on
`subagent_end` but explicitly deferred thread + message-row cleanup as a
known gap. Heavy fan-out workloads (parallel explore subagents, large
review/migration runs, A2A delegations that spawn many short-lived
subagents) therefore kept accumulating per-spawn thread rows and their
message history under the resource's MemoryStorage, even when the
session record was already gone.

Fix:

- In `spawn-subagent-tool.ts`, after the existing best-effort
  `harness.deleteSession({sessionId, resourceId})`, also call
  `harness.threads.delete({resourceId, threadId: child.threadId})`.
  Cleans BOTH the fresh-path thread (minted by
  `harness.session({threadId: {fresh: true}, ...})`) and the forked-path
  thread (cloned by `harness.threads.clone(...)`) — both belong
  exclusively to this child.
- Mirror the same `threads.delete` call at the workspace-tool-construction
  early-return path so partial-spawn failures don't leak a thread either.
- Both calls remain `.catch(() => {})` best-effort. `harness.threads.delete`
  refuses in setups where a separate session-storage override has marked
  the thread as externally-attached (harness.ts:3433 guard); in those
  environments the session row is still cleaned, and the worst case is a
  leftover thread + messages — strictly better than the pre-fix state.
- `retain: true` continues to suppress both deletions for subagents
  intended for re-attach.

Tests in `session.spawn-subagent.test.ts`:
- Default ephemeral spawn → asserts `harness.threads.delete` was called
  with `{resourceId, threadId}` matching the child (not the parent).
- `retain: true` → asserts `harness.threads.delete` is NOT called.
- Workspace-tool-construction early-return path → asserts
  `harness.threads.delete` IS called for the partially-created child.

Known gap still deferred: when `harness.threads.clone(...)` succeeds and
`harness.session(...)` then throws (the outer catch in spawn-subagent-tool),
no `child` exists and no thread cleanup runs. Narrow window; will land
with the broader clone-without-session cleanup pass.

commits.messageBody:
`EventEmitter.subscribe()` returns an unsubscribe closure that mutates
`this.listeners` via `splice()`. The dispatcher iterates the same array
with `for...of`, so a listener that calls its own (or a sibling's)
`unsubscribe()` synchronously during dispatch shifts later entries down
under the cursor — the listener that occupied the freed slot is skipped
for the in-flight emit.

This is a latent correctness bug today and a hard blocker for the
upcoming terminal-event work: subscribers commonly unsubscribe
on `session_closed`/`session_evicted`/`session_deleted`, which would
cause sibling listeners to miss the terminal notification.

Fix: iterate a one-shot copy (`[...this.listeners]`) so the live array
mutation cannot perturb the in-flight dispatch.

Two regression tests pin the contract:
- Self-unsubscribe during dispatch delivers to all siblings on the
  current emit; the removed listener is skipped on subsequent emits.
- Cross-listener unsubscribe encodes snapshot semantics: a listener
  removed by another listener mid-dispatch still receives the in-flight
  event (snapshot was already taken), and is excluded from the next
  emit.

Plain Array snapshot (not Set) preserves duplicate-listener semantics
since `subscribe()` is array-append. No public-API change.
commits.messageHeadline: fix(core/harness): snapshot EventEmitter listeners before dispatch

commits.messageBody:
… event

`EventEmitter` mints sequences starting at `0` (events.ts: post-increment
on emit). Storage backends — in-memory, libsql, pg — filter with
`event.sequence > afterSequence`. Together, this means callers need
`afterSequence: -1` to receive sequence `0`. But the public
`Session.listEventsAfter()` validation rejected `afterSequence < 0`,
making the first event unreachable through the documented API.

Relax the lower bound to `-1`. Storage is unchanged because all
backends already use the strict-greater semantics. `Harness.listSessionEventsAfter()`
needs no change either — it forwards `opts.afterSequence` straight to
storage with no validation.

Regression tests pin the contract end-to-end:
- `Session.listEventsAfter({afterSequence: -1})` returns sequence 0;
  the same call with `afterSequence: 0` excludes sequence 0 and yields
  one fewer row.
- `Harness.listSessionEventsAfter({afterSequence: -1})` returns
  sequence 0 (pass-through path).
- `afterSequence: -2` still rejected with a clear error.

This is the cursor-shape prerequisite for the upcoming replay
bridge: "from the beginning" must be a first-class request, not a hack.
commits.messageHeadline: fix(core/harness/v1): allow afterSequence=-1 to replay from the first…

commits.messageBody:
…play APIs

Before this change, the four replay-aware public APIs leaked the
storage-internal `HarnessStorageSessionEventReplayUnsupportedError`
when the storage adapter did not support the durable event ledger.
That error is not in the public error registry and is not what server
routes, A2A `tasks/resubscribe`, headless workers, or any other
consumer should catch.

The Linear scope addition spells this out: when an adapter
declares `sessionEventReplay: false`, replay-aware subscribe paths
must surface an explicit, typed error — not degrade silently and not
leak storage internals.

This commit introduces `HarnessEventReplayUnsupportedError`
(public error code `harness.event_replay_unsupported`) and raises it
from all four public surfaces:

- `Session.getEventReplayState()`
- `Session.listEventsAfter()`
- `Harness.getSessionEventReplayState()`
- `Harness.listSessionEventsAfter()`

Each surface uses two layers:

1. Upfront capability check — read `storage.capabilities().sessionEventReplay`
   and throw the typed error immediately when the adapter declares no
   replay support.

2. Defense-in-depth try/catch — translate any
   `HarnessStorageSessionEventReplayUnsupportedError` that slips through
   (e.g. an adapter that declares replay but throws on a specific call
   shape, or that develops a future regression). Preserves the typed
   contract even for misdeclared adapters.

Regression tests pin both branches:
- A NoReplayHarness subclass (overrides the three replay methods back
  to the base no-ops, flipping the capability prototype-detection to
  false) exercises the upfront-check path on all four surfaces.
- A stubbed-throw test forces the storage-internal error from a
  replay-declared adapter and verifies all four surfaces still
  translate to the typed public error.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): typed HarnessEventReplayUnsupportedError on re…

commits.messageBody:
…son: error

Five of the seven `agent_end` emit sites in session.ts collapsed
`full.finishReason === 'error'` into `reason: 'complete'` via a 2-way
ternary, hiding failure from durable replay and remote stream
consumers. The TUI dispatcher, headless exit code, A2A
`tasks/resubscribe`, and any other downstream consumer that reads
`agent_end.reason` would treat a failed run as a successful one.

Acceptance criterion #5 requires completed/failed stream
semantics to be observable. This commit standardizes a single
`mapFinishToAgentEndReason()` helper that does the deliberately lossy
3-way projection (`'suspended' | 'error' | 'complete'`) and applies it
at every emit site so the contract is uniform.

The helper's docstring makes the projection lossy by design — any
future richer taxonomy (timeout vs provider error vs aborted) belongs
on a new `agent_end` payload field or sibling event shape, not buried
inside this helper.

Regression tests cover all three turn paths:
- `message()` — direct emit at session.ts:3565.
- `queue()` — drains a queued turn through the post-run finalization
  emit site.
- resume after suspension — exercises the resumed-run emit site at
  session.ts:6665 (where the previous implementation already used a
  3-way ternary, but unification must not regress it).

Internal session callers of `agent_end.reason` were audited:
`_runGoalJudge` gates on `turn.finishReason === 'suspended'` directly,
not on `agent_end.reason`, so the helper change is a no-op for
internal behavior. The downstream change is only visible to external
event consumers (TUI, headless, A2A) — which is the point.
commits.messageHeadline: fix(core/harness/v1): propagate agent error finishes as agent_end.rea…

commits.messageBody:
Close and evict already emit `session_closed` / `session_evicted`
before the in-memory emitter is torn down. Hard-delete had no parity
event — subscribers saw the session go silent without a terminal
signal, breaking durable replay / remote stream consumers that need
to distinguish "session closed but record still exists" from "session
record gone forever".

This commit adds a `session_deleted` event with reason `'requested' |
'cascade'`. The root of the requested delete (tree[0]) gets
`'requested'`; every descendant cleaned up alongside it as part of
the subtree cascade gets `'cascade'`.

Emission ordering:
- After `storage.deleteSessions()` / `storage.deleteSession()`
  succeeds, so we never claim a delete that failed (atomic-failure
  recovery branch still emits only after verifying the row is gone).
- Before `live._markDeleted()` tears down the Session's emitter, so
  any session-level subscribers still receive the final lifecycle.

Coverage rules in `_markDeletedSession`:
- Live Session present + bridge still subscribed: emit via
  `live._emit()`; harness subscribers see it through the bridge.
- Live Session present + bridge already torn down (force-delete
  cascades close-first, which tears the bridge before
  `_deleteClosedTree` runs): emit on the session emitter for
  session-level subscribers AND directly on the harness emitter
  (with `sessionId` override) for harness-level subscribers, since
  the bridge can no longer forward.
- No live Session: emit only on the harness emitter (the session-
  level subscribers were torn down with the Session during close).

The emit is wrapped in a best-effort try/catch — the terminal
lifecycle must never block the delete path.

Regression tests cover:
- Normal close-then-delete: reason `'requested'` reaches harness
  subscribers (no-live path).
- Force-delete: reason `'requested'` reaches harness subscribers even
  though the bridge was torn down by the implicit close.
- Cascade: parent reason `'requested'` + child reason `'cascade'`
  when deleting a parent with closed descendants.

No double-delivery in any path — verified by tracing the live/bridge
branching against the close+delete lifecycle.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): emit session_deleted on hard-delete lifecycle

commits.messageBody:
Adds the production entry point for replay-then-live event consumption:
server routes, A2A `tasks/resubscribe`, headless workers, and IDE
extensions that need durable resume semantics without reinventing the
catch-up logic.

`bridgeReplayAndLive(harness, opts)` is an `AsyncGenerator<HarnessEvent>`
that:

  1. Subscribes to the live harness emitter first (no event loss
     during the replay phase).
  2. Optionally reads stored events from `opts.sinceCursor`
     (`'beginning'` | `{epoch, afterSequence}`) to the current head,
     paging by `opts.replayPageSize` (default 256).
  3. Switches to live, deduping the overlap by sequence so no event is
     delivered twice and none is missed.
  4. Filters by `opts.sessionId` so a single bridge only sees its
     session's events.

Composes the existing public surfaces (`Harness.subscribe`,
`Harness.getSessionEventReplayState`, `Harness.listSessionEventsAfter`)
— no private-field access into Harness or Session.

Typed errors with `harness.event_replay_*` codes registered in
`HARNESS_PUBLIC_ERROR_CODES` so transport adapters can map them to
wire shapes:

- `HarnessEventReplayStaleCursorError` — cursor older than oldest
  stored sequence; data loss unrecoverable.
- `HarnessEventReplayEpochMismatchError` — emitter regenerated since
  cursor was issued.
- `HarnessEventReplayFutureCursorError` — cursor ahead of newest
  stored sequence.
- `HarnessEventReplayBufferOverflowError` — live buffer exceeded
  `maxBufferedLive` (default 1000) during slow replay catch-up.
- `HarnessEventReplayAbortedError` — `opts.signal` aborted.

Cancellation: `opts.signal` is the canonical mechanism (immediate
cleanup via finally). `for await ... break` also cleans up but only
after the next yield point — pure async-generator semantics, documented
in the helper's docstring.

Bridge correctness pinned by 12 tests:
- 'beginning' replay covers every sequence from 0.
- Resume from a mid-sequence cursor yields strictly after it.
- Epoch mismatch / stale cursor / future cursor each throw the typed
  error.
- No-cursor path skips replay, starts from live.
- Replay → live dedupe: monotonic sequences, no duplicates, no gap.
- Cross-session events filtered out.
- Signal abort mid-iteration + already-aborted-signal cases.
- Buffer overflow under tight capacity during catch-up.
- Post-yield consumer break unsubscribes the harness listener.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): add bridgeReplayAndLive replay-aware event stream

commits.messageBody:
Adds `projectHarnessEventForPublicView` and wires it into
`bridgeReplayAndLive` via a new `publicView` option. The projector is
the canonical filter for events that must be safe to forward to a
remote/untrusted consumer (HTTP SSE, A2A `tasks/resubscribe`,
channel webhooks). In-process callers default to raw events — full
fidelity drives the local UI.

Default redaction policy. Strips every event field that can carry
secrets, user-supplied schema, or model-generated rationale:

- Tool I/O: `tool_input_delta.argsTextDelta`, `tool_start.args`,
  `tool_end.result`, `tool_update.partialResult`,
  `shell_output.output`.
- Subagent: `subagent_start.task`, `subagent_text_delta.delta`,
  `subagent_tool_start.args`, `subagent_tool_end.output`,
  `subagent_end.output`.
- Observational memory: `om_observation_end` (observations,
  currentTask, suggestedResponse), `om_reflection_end.observations`,
  `om_buffering_end.observations`, the `error` field on every
  `om_*_failed` shape, `om_thread_title_updated` titles.
- Threads: titles on `thread_created` / `thread_renamed` /
  `thread_cloned`; `thread_settings_changed.patch` and
  `.removedKeys`.
- Goal: `goal_set.goal`, `goal_judged.decision`,
  `goal_done.reason`.
- State: `state_changed.changedKeys`.
- Workspace: `workspace_error.error.message` (preserves
  `error.name` so consumers can still route on error class).
- Tasks: every `task_updated.tasks[].content` / `.activeForm`.
- Custom events (`<ns>.<name>` shape, non-reserved): `payload`
  redacted. Detection uses the shared `isCustomEventType` predicate
  so a future reserved dotted-namespace event cannot accidentally
  trip the custom path.

Preserves:
- Every id (`id`, `sessionId`, `runId`, `toolCallId`, …).
- Every lifecycle reason / status (`isError`, `finishReason`,
  `reason`, `phase`).
- Assistant `message_update.delta` — the user-visible streaming
  text; redacting it would defeat streaming UX. Subagent
  `subagent_text_delta.delta` is redacted because subagent
  transcripts may include tool transcripts not intended for a
  remote consumer; a transport can opt back in via `opts.redactor`.

Purity contract: input never mutated, return value is always a
fresh shallow clone — caller / redactor cannot mutate the caller's
event.

Customization: `PublicViewProjectionOptions.redactor` runs AFTER
the default policy. The redactor receives the projected event and
may return a tightened version, a loosened version (e.g. un-redact
an allowlisted tool's args), or `null` to drop the event from the
public stream entirely. The bridge advances its dedupe cursor on
dropped events so the live phase still cannot deliver them twice.

Wired into the bridge:
- `BridgeReplayOptions.publicView: boolean` — when true, every
  replay row AND every live event is projected before yielding.
- `BridgeReplayOptions.publicViewOptions?: PublicViewProjectionOptions`
  — forwarded to the projector per call.

24 new tests pin the default policy (every redaction listed
above), the customization seam (allowlist + drop), and the bridge
integration (replay + live + dedupe on drop).
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): public-view event projector + bridge opt-in

commits.messageBody:
Adds the missing primitive between in-flight session events and the
durable record of work products — plans, diffs, test reports,
screenshots, patches, review output. Artifacts are immutable,
versioned, content-addressed via the existing attachment store, and
queryable through a typed public API.

Storage substrate (`storage/domains/harness/{base,types,inmemory}.ts`):

- `HarnessArtifactRecord` — `{ artifactId, lineageRootId,
  parentArtifactId?, version, artifactType, attachmentId, mimeType,
  bytes, sha256, schemaUri?, createdAt, createdBy, metadata? }`.
  `lineageRootId === artifactId` for v1; subsequent writes set it from
  the parent so lineage walks are O(1) on the (sessionId, lineageRootId)
  index.
- `HarnessStorageCapabilities.harnessArtifacts: boolean` with
  prototype-detection over the four artifact methods, mirroring the
  workspace-journal / event-replay capability pattern.
- Base stubs throw `HarnessStorageArtifactsUnsupportedError` — adapters
  fail loud rather than silently degrading.
- In-memory implementation enforces: caller-supplied `artifactId`
  uniqueness; `parentArtifactId` lives in the same `(sessionId,
  resourceId)`; CAS guard on `(lineageRootId, version)`; attachment
  must exist on the same session, with `mimeType` / `bytes` / `sha256`
  copied from the attachment record so artifacts capture canonical
  immutable values.
- Session hard-delete now drops matching artifact rows alongside
  attachment bytes (and `dangerouslyClearAll` too), so deleted
  sessions cannot leave dangling rows pointing at vanished
  attachments.

Six typed storage errors (`HarnessStorageArtifactsUnsupportedError`,
`HarnessStorageArtifactNotFoundError`,
`HarnessStorageArtifactLineageMismatchError`,
`HarnessStorageArtifactVersionConflictError`,
`HarnessStorageArtifactDuplicateIdError`,
`HarnessStorageArtifactAttachmentMissingError`) map to six public
typed errors registered in `HARNESS_PUBLIC_ERROR_CODES`.

Public API (`harness.ts`):

- `harness.artifacts.write({ sessionId, resourceId, threadId,
  artifactId, artifactType, attachmentId, parentArtifactId?,
  schemaUri?, createdBy?, metadata? })`. Caller uploads bytes via
  `harness.attachments.upload()` first, then passes the
  resulting `attachmentId` to `write`. Hash/MIME/bytes are
  authoritative-from-attachment, not caller-supplied.
- `harness.artifacts.get({ sessionId, resourceId, artifactId })`.
- `harness.artifacts.list({ sessionId, resourceId, artifactType?,
  limit?, cursor? })`.
- `harness.artifacts.versions({ sessionId, resourceId, artifactId })`
  — walks the lineage from any anchor in version order.

`artifact_created` event:

- New shape `{ type: 'artifact_created'; artifactId; artifactType;
  lineageRootId; parentArtifactId?; version; mimeType; sha256; bytes }`.
- Added to `HarnessEvent` union and `RESERVED_EVENT_TYPES`.
- Emitted on the live session's emitter when a Session handle exists
  (bridge forwards to harness-level subscribers); falls back to the
  harness emitter with a `sessionId` override when the caller writes
  against a closed/non-live session (e.g. a server route).
- Public-view projection passes through unchanged — every field is
  safe metadata (no payload bytes).

Why no `taskId` / `runId` columns: the canonical Task / Run primitives
have not been defined yet. Consumers that need to associate artifacts
with a task or run use the opaque `metadata` map until the canonical
schema lands.

Why no inline content: attachments already provide MIME + sha256 +
immutable IDs + references; inline JSON would inflate row size and
duplicate the redaction policy that public-view applies to attachment
references. The single-source-of-truth content live in the attachment
store.

Why no approval/denial or workspace-mutation evidence: inbox response
receipts and the workspace action journal already cover those
categories. The artifact substrate is the missing piece, not the
existing evidence surfaces.

Comprehensive coverage:
- Storage tests pin write/load/list/versions, lineage CAS, duplicate
  rejection, missing-attachment rejection, capability-false adapter,
  capability prototype detection.
- Harness-level tests pin every public surface against typed-error
  translation, `artifact_created` reaching both session-level and
  harness-level subscribers via the bridge, the no-live-session emit
  fallback, and cascade cleanup on hard-delete.

12 storage tests + 13 harness tests; 1010 total tests across harness/v1
+ storage domain pass; typecheck clean; codex pass ×2 + coderabbit
clean before commit.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): durable artifact substrate

commits.messageBody:
…lyProfile

Adds the named-policy-profile substrate that makes Harness v1 safe to
expose beyond a trusted local CLI. Server/A2A/channel entrypoints can
now declare a non-YOLO baseline (read-only review, approval-gated
patch, ci-fixer) that the local CLI's lenient defaults cannot bypass.

Module `harness/v1/permission-profiles.ts`:

- `HarnessPermissionProfile` — purely declarative bundle of per-category
  baseline + per-tool overrides + session grants + operator-visible tags.
- `HARNESS_PERMISSION_PROFILES` — frozen registry of 4 presets:
  - `readOnlyReview`: read=allow; edit/execute/mcp/other=deny. Tagged
    `remote-safe`.
  - `approvalGatedPatch`: read=allow; everything else=ask. Tagged
    `remote-safe`.
  - `ciFixer`: read=allow; everything else=ask. Tagged `remote-safe`.
    Args-aware allowlisting deferred (the runtime resolver hook drops
    per-call args before reaching the session resolver, so any
    declarative `execute: allow` would over-permit — operators layer
    a workspace-policy rule for the specific commands their CI uses).
  - `trustedLocalYolo`: every category=allow + grants every category.
    Tagged `local-only` — never default for a remote route.
- `rulesFromProfile(profile, opts?)` / `grantsFromProfile(profile)`
  helpers, reusable by future surfaces (subagent profile binding,
  per-actor grants).

Public API:

- `session.permissions.applyProfile({ profileName,
  preserveCallerDenies? })`. REPLACE semantics for both
  `permissionRules` and `sessionGrants` — a profile reset never carries
  stale rules forward when the posture changes. `preserveCallerDenies:
  true` overlays only `policy === 'deny'` entries from the prior rule
  set on top of the new profile so safety overrides survive.
- `harness.permissions.profiles.{get,list}` — static registry lookup.
- `harness.permissions.applyProfile({ sessionId, resourceId,
  profileName, preserveCallerDenies? })` — convenience wrapper for
  server-route callers that don't already hold a Session handle.
- `HarnessPermissionProfileNotFoundError` (public-error registry).

Event `permission_profile_applied`:

- `{ profileName; previousProfileName?; mode: 'replace' |
  'replace-preserve-denies'; categories: ...; preservedToolDenies }`.
- Emitted on the session emitter so subscribers + the durable event
  ledger record an auditable snapshot of every policy reset.
- Public-view passes through unchanged (no payload bytes; safe to
  forward to remote subscribers).

Storage:

- `SessionRecord.appliedPermissionProfile?: string` — opaque slot for
  the last-applied profile name, used by audit/replay consumers and
  by the event payload's `previousProfileName`. Legacy records (no
  profile applied) load as `undefined`.

Anti-drift / accuracy:

- Every preset populates every {@link ToolCategory} explicitly. A
  profile that left a category undefined would fall through to the
  harness-level `defaultPermissionPolicy`, breaking `readOnlyReview`
  on a harness configured with `'allow'` as the default.
- Workspace-policy fragments are intentionally NOT in this slice —
  the harness workspace policy is constructor-only and a
  session-scoped overlay is a separate substrate.
- `_permApplyProfile` computes every snapshot from `prev` INSIDE the
  serialized `_flushUpdate` callback. The earlier draft read
  `this._record` synchronously above the await, which would have
  dropped a caller-set deny added between the synchronous read and
  the queued flush (see the concurrent-setPolicy regression test).

16 tests pin the contract:
- Every preset populates all 5 categories.
- Each preset's category map matches the documented posture.
- Replace semantics drop stale caller grants/rules.
- `preserveCallerDenies: true` keeps `deny` entries; default mode
  drops them.
- `permission_profile_applied` reaches both session-level and
  harness-level subscribers.
- `previousProfileName` set on second consecutive apply.
- Unknown profile name → `HarnessPermissionProfileNotFoundError`.
- Resolution semantics: `readOnlyReview` denies edit even when the
  harness `defaultPermissionPolicy` is `'allow'`.
- `harness.permissions.profiles.{get,list}` + the
  harness-level `applyProfile` convenience.
- Concurrent `setPolicy` (queued first) + `applyProfile` with
  `preserveCallerDenies: true` (queued second) — the regression test
  for the race the earlier draft would have failed.

Deferred to follow-up slices on this branch:
- Per-actor / per-channel grants (SessionRecord schema change).
- Subagent profile binding (subagent type config wires a profile,
  applied on spawn).
- Sandbox access request API.
- Args-aware permission resolution.

1026 tests pass across harness/v1 + storage. Codex pass × 4 +
CodeRabbit clean.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): permission profile primitive + 4 presets + app…

commits.messageBody:
…ions

One session can be served by many EXTERNAL callers concurrently —
multiple A2A agents on a shared task, multiple channel users on a
Slack thread, the CLI plus a server route on the same workspace.
This commit makes per-actor grants a first-class primitive so each
caller can hold a distinct grant posture without affecting the
others.

Storage:

- `SessionRecord.actorGrants?: Record<string, SessionGrants>` —
  keys are stable composite actor identities. Undefined on legacy
  records (the resolver falls back to `sessionGrants`, preserving
  every pre-change behavior).

Types:

- `HarnessActorIdentity { kind: 'a2a' | 'channel' | 'cli' |
  'server'; id; displayName? }`. Subagents are NOT a kind here —
  internal execution topology stays in the action journal payload
  (`actor.source: 'subagent'` + `parentSessionId`), confirmed by
  codex + agy. A `'subagent'` kind would mix permission identity
  with execution structure and be unstable across ephemeral child
  sessions.
- `actorKey(actor): string` — deterministic `${kind}:${id}` stable
  across processes.

Resolver:

- `Session._resolveToolPermissionPolicy(toolName, opts?: { args?;
  actor? })`. When an actor is supplied, the resolver checks
  `actorGrants[actorKey(actor)]` BEFORE falling back to
  `sessionGrants`. The signature accepts `args?` for the imminent
  Slice 5 wire-up so the signature ships once, not twice.

Wire-up:

- `HarnessRequestContext.actor?: HarnessActorIdentity` — populated
  by the harness slot from persisted channel context via a new
  `deriveActorFromChannel(channel)` helper. The composite key
  includes provider, tenant, channel, and platformUserId so the
  same Slack user id on two different workspaces / providers
  resolves to distinct actor keys (anti-collision).
- The slot's `resolveToolPermission` callback prefers a
  caller-supplied actor and falls back to the channel-derived
  actor, so every channel-originated tool call routes through the
  actor grant overlay automatically without dispatcher changes.

Public API:

- `session.permissions.grantTool({ toolName, actor? })`,
  `grantCategory`, `revokeTool`, `revokeCategory` —
  actor-optional. Session-level paths are byte-compatible with the
  pre-change API.
- `getGrants({ actor? })` — with an actor, returns ONLY that
  actor's overlay (not the union). Documented overlay semantics.

Events:

- `permission_granted` and `permission_revoked` event shapes
  extended with optional `actor?: HarnessActorIdentity`.
  Subscribers and audit consumers can now distinguish an actor
  overlay change from a baseline session-grant change — without
  this, mirrored permission state would drift silently.

Race-safety:

- The pre-existing outer pre-check + serialized flush pattern was
  vulnerable to a concurrent-call race (two identical grants both
  pass the outer check, both rebuild the map, both emit).
  Reworked to: dedupe inside the `_flushUpdate` callback against
  `prev`, gate emit on a `changed` closure flag set only when the
  callback actually returned a different record.
- Applied uniformly across all 8 mutator paths (4 session-level +
  4 actor-scoped). Two regression tests pin the no-double-emit and
  no-double-append behavior under `Promise.all([grant, grant])`.

`applyProfile` clears every per-actor grant alongside the
session-level reset inside the same `_flushUpdate`. A profile
transition cannot leave stale per-caller privilege from a stronger
prior posture (e.g. an `a2a:malicious-agent` grant surviving a
switch to `readOnlyReview`).

17 tests pin the contract:
- `actorKey` composite produces deterministic keys; anti-collision
  for same user id on different tenants.
- `deriveActorFromChannel` returns undefined for missing context,
  builds the full composite when present.
- Per-actor grant suppresses `ask` for that actor only; other
  actors / session-level resolution unchanged.
- Per-actor revoke removes only that actor; cleanup of empty
  entries prevents map bloat.
- Session-level path remains byte-compatible with pre-change
  callers.
- `applyProfile` drops actorGrants; subsequent resolution against
  the previously-granted actor returns the profile's per-category
  posture.
- `permission_granted` / `permission_revoked` events carry the
  actor identity on the actor-scoped path; omitted on session-
  level path (legacy shape).
- Harness-level subscribers see the event with the actor
  preserved (bridge forwards verbatim).
- Two concurrent identical grants emit ONE event (race-safe).

Codex pass × 4 + CodeRabbit pass × 3 all clean before commit. 1041
tests pass across harness/v1 + storage.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): per-actor permission grants on Harness v1 sess…

commits.messageBody:
…lver

The harness slot's `resolveToolPermission` hook receives per-call
`args` from the agent runtime's tool dispatcher (`tool-call-step.ts`
passes `args ?? {}`), but the slot previously dropped them on the
floor when forwarding to `Session._resolveToolPermissionPolicy`. The
session resolver signature already accepted `args?` from the
permission-profiles slice — only the wire-up was missing.

This commit closes the gap: the slot now forwards `params.args` to
the session resolver when present. The session-level resolver does
not introspect args itself today — workspace-policy rules
(file/command/network/mcp) produce the args-aware verdict recorded
on the action journal post-execution — but the signature is now
plumbed so a future profile-side resolver hook, operator override,
or pre-execution-gate slice can read them without another wire-up
commit.

Docstring updates correct an inaccurate claim about workspace policy
being a pre-execution gate. It is currently a post-execution journal
overlay; that's documented in the permission-profiles module
docstring + the slot wire-up comment + the test comments so future
contributors don't mistake the audit-side verdict for enforcement.

One regression test pins the contract: `_resolveToolPermissionPolicy`
accepts `opts.args` and resolution still returns the declarative
verdict (no args-based override applied yet, by design).

Codex pass × 5 + CodeRabbit clean before commit. 1042 tests pass.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): wire per-call args through the permission reso…

commits.messageBody:
A subagent type can now declare a permission profile that the spawn
path applies to every child session it creates. `allowedWorkspaceTools`
only filtered the workspace tools visible to the model — it did not
prevent the child from calling custom tools, MCP tools, channel
actions, or any non-workspace tool. This commit closes that gap:

- `SubagentDefinition.profile?: HarnessPermissionProfileName` (the
  type is imported directly from `./permission-profiles` so a profile
  added or removed in the registry catches drift at every use site
  through the compiler — no local duplicate union, no silent skew).

- `spawn-subagent` applies the profile to the child session AFTER
  installing the parent's subagent-event bridge and BEFORE the first
  agent turn runs. The resulting `permission_profile_applied` event
  reaches the harness emitter (with the child session's id), so
  audit consumers see the lifecycle without any new event shape.

- Apply-profile failure tears down the bridge subscriber (`unsub()`)
  before closing/deleting the child session, mirroring the workspace-
  tools init failure path so the parent never holds a dangling
  listener on a closed child emitter.

Why profile binding matters: a true read-only or approval-gated
subagent posture requires the child resolver to gate EVERY tool call,
not just workspace ones. Apply-profile drops both `permissionRules`
and `sessionGrants` in a replace-not-merge reset, so the child cannot
escalate via session-level grants either. Per-actor grants from the
parent's channel context are NOT carried into the child — subagent
execution is its own permission scope by design.

3 tests pin the contract:
- A subagent type with `profile: 'readOnlyReview'` produces a child
  session whose persisted rules and grants reflect the profile
  (read=allow, every other category=deny, empty grants).
- A `permission_profile_applied` event for the child fires on the
  harness emitter (audit-visible).
- A subagent type without `profile` leaves the child session's rules
  untouched — legacy behavior preserved byte-for-byte.

Codex pass × 2 + CodeRabbit clean before commit. 1047 tests pass
across harness/v1 + storage.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): subagent type profile binding

commits.messageBody:
A tool can now request structured sandbox-level approval from inside
its execute() body via `ctx.registerSandboxAccess(...)`. The harness
records a typed `pendingResume.kind: 'sandbox-access'`, emits a
`sandbox_access_requested` event, and suspends the turn until the
approver calls `session.respondToSandboxAccess({approved, reason?})`.

This is the APPROVAL workflow primitive — the harness itself does not
enforce sandbox access at the OS / process layer. Sandbox-aware
runtimes wire `ctx.registerSandboxAccess` into their access-request
path so every privileged operation suspends with structured evidence
on the action journal + event ledger.

Schema:

- `PendingResume.kind` extended with `'sandbox-access'`. The
  `payload.sandboxAccess` carries `{ semanticType, reason?, payload? }`
  where `semanticType ∈ 'file' | 'command' | 'network' | 'mcp' |
  'custom'`. The four non-custom values are aligned with
  `WorkspacePolicyActionKind` so existing workspace-policy renderers
  can render the prompt without a translation layer; `'custom'`
  covers caller-defined sandbox surfaces that don't fit the four.
- `InboxResponseResult.kind` union extended with `'sandbox-access'`
  so inbox-receipt callers can route on the discriminator.

Events:

- `SandboxAccessRequestedEvent { requestId, toolCallId, semanticType,
  reason?, payload? }` — emitted after pending-resume registration
  alongside the existing `suspension_required`.
- `SandboxAccessResolvedEvent { requestId, toolCallId, semanticType,
  approved }` — emitted from inside `_resume` AFTER the CAS commit
  on `pendingResume.resumedAt` AND before the tool resumes. Invalid
  responses, duplicate receipts, and concurrent losers do NOT emit
  this event, so audit consumers can treat it as an authoritative
  verdict.

Public + ctx API:

- `RegisterSandboxAccessParams` — tool-side request shape.
- `HarnessRequestContext.registerSandboxAccess?` — tool hook that
  delegates to `Session._registerSandboxAccess`.
- `Session.respondToSandboxAccess({ approved, reason? })` — routes
  through the same `_resume` machinery as every other respond-* API,
  inheriting itemId/responseId conflict handling, idempotency, and
  inbox-receipt support.

Anti-drift defenses landed in the implementation pass:

- Strict JSON validation on `params.payload` via the same
  `assertJsonValue` helper used by inbox-receipt admission hashing —
  rejects functions, Dates, NaN/Infinity, BigInt, sparse arrays, and
  class instances. Same validator across every harness surface that
  accepts a tool-supplied JSON payload.
- Idempotency check on re-registration matches the existing pending
  by either `toolCallId` OR `itemId` so a caller passing a custom
  `toolCallId` with the same `requestId` is still a no-op (not a
  throw).
- The `sandbox_access_resolved` event fires inside `_resume` after
  the CAS commit on `pendingResume.resumedAt`. Pre-CAS branches
  (duplicate receipt, mismatched item id, already-resumed pending)
  return before the emit, so audit consumers never see a false or
  duplicate resolved event.

9 tests pin the contract:
- Pending resume is registered with the supplied payload preserved
  verbatim through the round-trip.
- `sandbox_access_requested` and `suspension_required` fire on
  registration.
- Unknown semanticType / empty requestId / missing runId all raise
  `HarnessValidationError`.
- Non-JSON payload (function, Date, NaN) raises
  `HarnessValidationError` BEFORE persistence — no malformed value
  reaches storage or the event ledger.
- Idempotent re-registration with the same id is a no-op.
- `respondToSandboxAccess({approved})` emits
  `sandbox_access_resolved` with the verdict; works for both
  approve and deny paths.

Codex pass × 5 + CodeRabbit pass × 2 all clean before commit. 1055
tests pass across harness/v1 + storage.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): sandbox-access request API

commits.messageBody:
Frees the `Task` name for the upcoming canonical work-unit primitive.
The in-thread todo type and its schema were called `TaskItem` /
`taskItemSchema`; both are now `HarnessTodo` / `harnessTodoSchema`.

Scope is internal-only. None of the operator-visible surface changes:
the `task_write` / `task_check` tool ids stay, the persisted
thread-metadata key (`thread.metadata.mastra.tasks`) stays, and the
`task_updated` event-type wire string stays. Only the TS interface
element type on `TaskUpdatedEvent.tasks` is renamed.

No behavior change. Existing task-write / task-check tests pass
unchanged on the new names; the broader 973-test harness/v1 suite is
green.
commits.messageHeadline: refactor(core/harness/v1): rename TaskItem to HarnessTodo (prep)

commits.messageBody:
Type-only this slice. Defines `HarnessTask` and `HarnessRun` —
the canonical work-unit and execution-attempt primitives the
Harness v1 substrate uses across user / A2A / channel / CLI /
server / system entrypoints. Also ships `TaskIndexEntry`, the
durable mapping row shape (sessionId / runId / queuedItemId /
a2aTaskId) that the cancellation umbrella will materialize.

The file-level docblock includes the PF-631 §2 field-mapping
table — every pre-existing identifier (sessionId, threadId, runId,
admissionId, signalId, queuedItemId, workspaceActionId,
subagentSessionId, goalId, pendingResume.itemId, A2A task.id,
A2A task.contextId) is now documented against its canonical
primitive, so downstream code stops inventing parallel vocabulary.

No storage schema changes, no new APIs, no event types. The
contracts compile under the existing tsconfig; the seven-case
contracts.test.ts pins the field set so accidental renames or
widening break the build.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): add canonical Task / Run type contracts

commits.messageBody:
…pe rename

Two related changes against the canonical-contracts slice:

1. Renamed the storage union `OperationAdmissionEvidence` to
   `HarnessOperationAdmissionEvidence`. The body is unchanged —
   the rename only makes its narrow admission-only scope explicit
   so downstream readers don't confuse it with the broader public
   evidence shape. The old name stays exported as a `@deprecated`
   alias, so every existing import keeps compiling.

2. Added `HarnessEvidence`, a tagged-union public contract over
   the three durable proof rows a Harness v1 session can hand
   back: admission evidence (the storage union above), workspace
   journal entries, and inbox response receipts. The `evidenceKind`
   discriminator lets consumers narrow on one stable field instead
   of property-presence checks, which is what every current
   consumer already does (see `harness-wakeup-worker.ts`).

The two only existing callsites that referenced the old name by
type (`base.ts:42`, `base.ts:818`) are migrated to the new
canonical name. Adapter packages (libsql / pg / inmemory) keep
their structural typing through the deprecated alias and stay

Type-only. No writer paths touched, no storage rows mutated.
Pinned by ten cases in `contracts.test.ts`, and the storage +
session.message suites (131 tests) remain green on the alias.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): canonical HarnessEvidence union + admission ty…

commits.messageBody:
Surfaces the durable wait-point primitive under its canonical
PF-631 name on the public v1 entry point. `PendingResume` (already
shipping with five `kind`s after the sandbox-access slice) is the
internal/storage symbol; `PendingInteraction` is the public alias
the canonical-contracts ticket calls for.

Structurally identical — `PendingInteraction = PendingResume`.
No fields rename, no runtime change. One pin in contracts.test.ts
asserts the two names are freely interchangeable both directions.

Closes the four schema-side slices of PF-631 (rename of the
in-thread todo, canonical Task/Run types, evidence vocabulary
cleanup, and this alias). The fifth slice — boot-warn for unknown
HarnessConfig keys — lands separately and is independent of the
scheduler umbrella.

commits.messageBody:
…tion

Closes the last canonical-contracts slice: catch typo'd or stale
top-level config keys at boot instead of silently ignoring them.
The constructor walks `Object.keys(config)` once before reading
any field, and emits a structured `console.warn` for each key
that isn't in the locally-defined `HARNESS_CONFIG_KNOWN_KEYS` set
(every documented `HarnessConfigCommon` field plus the
`mastra` / `agents` / `storage` discriminants).

Warn-only on purpose — `HarnessConfigCommon` still carries the
`[key: string]: unknown` index signature, and removing it is a
typed-breaking change for downstream consumers that we explicitly
deferred during plan review. A future release can lift the warn
to a hard error once the migration window has passed.

Pinned by three cases in `harness.config-keys.test.ts`: one warn
per unknown key, no warn on a fully-known config, and an inverse
pin asserting every documented top-level key passes through
cleanly. The broader 128-test `harness.test.ts` suite is green
unchanged.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): warn on unknown HarnessConfig keys at construc…

commits.messageBody:
Adds `Session.cancel({reason?, requestedBy?})` and
`Session.cancelQueuedItem({queuedItemId, reason?})` — the first
durable cancellation primitives on Harness v1 sessions. The
existing `Session.abort(...)` only cancels the in-flight turn's
AbortController; the new methods persist a `cancelRequest` marker
on SessionRecord so a crash mid-cancel cannot silently resurrect
the work.

Behavior:

  * `cancel(...)` is idempotent — the first call wins; the second
    is a no-op preserving the original reason / requestedAt /
    requestedBy.
  * Inside the same CAS write, every pending queued item is
    removed from `pendingQueue`. Their resolvers reject with
    `HarnessSessionCancelledError`, carrying the cancel reason.
  * After the marker is durable the in-flight turn is aborted via
    the existing controller.
  * Two new events: `task_cancellation_requested` (one per cancel)
    and `queue_item_cancelled` (one per removed queued turn).
  * The harness heartbeat short-circuits lease renewal when the
    marker is present — cancelled sessions age out naturally and
    release their lease instead of being kept alive forever.

`cancelQueuedItem(...)` is the fine-grained sibling — drops a
single queued turn without marking the session-wide
`cancelRequest`. Idempotent over unknown ids.

Storage:

  * New `cancel_request` JSONB column on the harness sessions
    table (constants.ts). The libsql + pg adapters declare it in
    their column lists and ifNotExists alterTable migration, so
    existing deployments pick it up at next `init()`.
  * In-memory + libsql + pg adapters all round-trip the field.

10 cases in session.cancel.test.ts pin the primitive end-to-end:
persistence, idempotency, event emission, queue resolver
rejection, in-flight abort delegation, and the heartbeat
short-circuit. The broader 997-test harness/v1 suite + the
libsql harness suite (789 tests) remain green unchanged.

Subagent-tree propagation and resume gating ship in the next
sub-slice (PF-665 part B).
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): durable session cancellation primitive

commits.messageBody:
Closes PF-665. The cancellation primitive landed in the previous
commit now propagates through the live subagent tree and gates
the resume path.

Subagent propagation:

  * After `Session.cancel(...)` commits its CAS write, it walks
    `_activeSubagents` and calls `child.cancel({reason})` on every
    live child. Recursion is automatic — each child's own cancel
    walks its own subagent tree.
  * Children see `requestedBy === parent.id` on their
    `cancelRequest`, so audit can reconstruct the cascade.
  * Cancellation does NOT propagate upward. A subagent cancelling
    itself cannot take down the parent. Tested.
  * Child lookups go through a new
    `Harness._internalGetLiveSession(sessionId)` shim — `Session`
    can't reach `_liveSessions` directly because the field is
    private.

Resume gating:

  * Inside `_resume(...)`'s CAS callback, if a durable
    `cancelRequest` committed BEFORE the resume reached the CAS
    and the pending interaction wasn't already marked `resumedAt`,
    refuse to mark `resumedAt` and surface
    `HarnessSessionCancelledError` to the caller.
  * Already-resumed paths are unaffected — the abort path handles
    in-flight cancellation separately. The new precedence rule is
    "cancel wins before resumedAt is stamped".
  * Applies uniformly to every public resume entry point
    (`respondToToolApproval`, `respondToToolSuspension`,
    `respondToQuestion`, `respondToPlanApproval`,
    `respondToSandboxAccess`).

Three new cases in session.cancel.test.ts pin the cascade
direction, the no-bubble-up guarantee, and the resume rejection.
The full 1000-test harness/v1 suite stays green.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): cancellation tree propagation + resume gating

commits.messageBody:
Three correctness fixes from deep review of the cancellation
primitive landed in the previous two commits:

1. Concurrent cancels no longer double-emit or double-abort.
   The post-commit emit / abort / subagent-propagation path now
   only runs on the CAS winner — losers see `cancelRequest`
   already set inside the callback, return `prev`, and bail out
   via a `wasWinner` flag check.

2. The currently-running queued turn (`_currentQueuedItemId`) is
   no longer removed from `pendingQueue` during cancel. It stays
   in place so the existing abort/failure flow can settle it
   normally. Without this guard, `isBusy()` and
   `lookupQueueResult()` saw the active turn vanish under them.

3. `queueAdmissionReceipts` for cancelled items are marked
   `failed` (with the durable cancel error projected via
   `projectHarnessPublicError`) in the same CAS write that
   clears them from `pendingQueue`. Without this, follow-up
   admissions saw `status: 'queued'` and treated the receipt as
   live work, defeating dedupe.

Same active-head + failed-receipt treatment applied to
`cancelQueuedItem(...)` so the single-item path doesn't drift
from the session-wide path.

Plus a tiny audit fix: `HarnessSessionCancelledError` now has
its public wire code (`harness.session_cancelled`) wired in
`HARNESS_PUBLIC_ERROR_CODES`, so SDK / route layers serializing
the error see a stable code instead of `undefined`.

Three new cases on the cancel test file pin the fixes:
concurrent cancels emit exactly once, cancelled items show
`failed` status on their receipts, and the active head is kept
across cancel. The full 1000-test harness/v1 suite is green.
commits.messageHeadline: fix(core/harness/v1): cancel race, active head, receipt audit gaps

commits.messageBody:
Adds two optional scheduling hints to `QueuedItem`:

  * `priority?: number` — higher values drain first; defaults to 0.
    Same-priority items keep their `enqueuedAt` order, so the
    legacy FIFO contract is preserved for callers that opt out.
  * `deadline?: number` — epoch ms past which the item must not
    start. The scheduler drops expired items, emits a new
    `queue_item_expired` event, marks the queue admission receipt
    `failed` (with `projectHarnessPublicError`), and rejects the
    original `session.queue(...)` promise — all in a single CAS
    write so a crash mid-drop is replay-safe.

The drain step itself stays head-of-line — the new
`_scheduleNextQueueHead()` runs once per drain iteration to
rotate the highest-priority survivor into `pendingQueue[0]`
before the existing recovery + post-run finalize code reads it.
That preserves the long-standing `pendingQueue[0]` invariant
across crash recovery.

Storage: no schema change — the new optional fields ride along
inside the existing `pending_queue` JSONB column.

Also strips a few stale internal-ticket references from code
comments that landed during earlier work; they should never have
shipped in the source tree.

Six new cases in `session.scheduler.test.ts` pin priority
rotation, FIFO tie-break, default-priority handling, deadline
expiry behavior + receipt audit, and the no-op contract on empty
queues. The full 1009-test harness/v1 suite stays green.
commits.messageHeadline: feat(core/harness/v1): priority + deadline on the durable queue

commits.messageBody:
… changeset

Two follow-up fixes from review of the prior priority/deadline
commit:

  * The no-op short-circuit in `_scheduleNextQueueHead` previously
    compared `nextQueue === queue`, which never matched because
    `nextQueue` was always a fresh array built from filtered
    survivors. Replaced with explicit "did anything change?"
    flags (`didExpire`, `rotated`, `receiptsChanged`) so a queue
    with nothing expired and the right head already in place
    skips the storage write instead of incurring an unnecessary
    version bump.

  * Rewrote the priority/deadline changeset in caller-facing
    terms (`session.queue({priority, deadline})`) with concrete
    usage examples for the two surfaces. The internal mechanics
    (storage column, CAS write) stay out of the customer-facing
    line.

The six scheduler tests still pin priority rotation, FIFO
tie-break, default-priority, deadline expiry, future-deadline
keep, and the empty-queue no-op.
